verdict: 2026-07-19 10:15 PDT — The phased compiler, stable L1 unit IDs, pinned sampling, and discriminated row-patch payloads are substantial improvements. The overall architecture is sound, but v1 is not yet a complete stable engine contract: it does not bind approval to a source snapshot, its patch union does not structurally distinguish compilation phases, and its semantic gate still permits schedules whose units or prices cannot be verified from the artifact.

findings.title: The approved plan is not bound to a source snapshot
findings.locus: SKILL.md Hard constraints and steps 2–6; plan-schema.json target/date
findings.claim: The artifact records only a mutable absolute target path and calendar date. It records neither a Git revision/tree state nor the exact evaluation instant, while churn uses the time-relative `git log --since='90 days ago'`. An unchanged repository can therefore compile differently as time passes, and the future engine can execute changed content at the same path after approval. That contradicts both “same repo state + same overrides ⇒ same plan” and “execute exactly that artifact.”
findings.alternative: Make source identity part of v1: record a revision/tree identifier, dirty-state policy or content fingerprint, and an ISO evaluation cutoff; derive the 90-day boundary from that stored cutoff. At execution, fail closed on source mismatch or explicitly check out the recorded revision.
findings.win: Binds one approval to one code state and one signal window, eliminating silent plan/content drift and one source of non-reproducible compilation.

findings.title: Patch phase is encoded in prose rather than the union
findings.claim: Pre-compilation file filters and post-compilation row edits share the same structural branches: `exclude=<glob>` becomes a generic `remove` selector, just as `<lens>:off` does, while `only=<glob>` becomes a generic `restrict` selector. The schema has no phase or file-filter discriminant, so replay must inspect the optional human token or infer intent from selector values; direct consult patches may have `token: null`. The supposedly normalized patch record therefore does not independently determine where it applies.
findings.alternative: Give file filtering its own discriminated branches, such as `{op: "exclude-files", glob}` and `{op: "only-files", glob}`, and reserve selector-based `remove`/`restrict` for compiled rows. Apply branches by their structural op, not token text.
findings.win: Removes token reparsing and phase inference, makes consult-origin filters replayable, and gives the compiler one unambiguous patch ordering model.

findings.title: The semantic gate does not prove unit or pricing executability
findings.locus: SKILL.md steps 5–6; plan-schema.json unitMap, rows, totals, assumptions
findings.claim: The named check verifies group counts, row run factors, light sampling, identity uniqueness, and aggregate sums, but it does not require standard/deep row `unitIds` to equal the scope registry, `units` to equal the full resolved scope size, `estTokens` to equal runs × the pricing constant, or wall clock to follow its formula. Pricing constants remain unstructured prose. L2/L3 synthetic IDs (`subsystem:<dir>`, `app`) also have no registry entry connecting them to source membership. Consequently schema plus semantic validation can accept an internally summed artifact that the engine must reinterpret or defensively validate.
findings.alternative: Use one registry for every executable scope, including subsystem/application membership and chunk boundaries; represent pricing constants structurally; extend the semantic check to enforce exact registry membership/order, full-scope `units`, sampling, token arithmetic, and wall-clock arithmetic.
findings.win: Rejects malformed or ambiguous schedules before approval and removes engine-side source resolution, pricing inference, and duplicate defensive validation.
